revision.text: {{draft}}{{PAGENAME}} is a Japanese primordial deity{{citation needed}} ==References==
{{draft}}{{PAGENAME}} is a Japanese primordial deity{{citation needed}} ==References==

{{draft}} [[File:Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|ウマシアシカビヒコヂ}} is a Japanese primordial deity. He was the fourth one to come into existence<ref>https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/</ref>
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |access-date=2023-03-22 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
==References==

{{draft}} [[File:Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|ウマシアシカビヒコヂ}} is a Japanese primordial deity. He was the fourth one to come into existence<ref>https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/</ref> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/wip/LW8jJ</ref>
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |access-date=2023-03-22 |website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor<ref name=":0" />
==References==

revision.text: {{AFC submission|d|npov|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|decliner=InterstellarGamer12321|declinets=20230323075459|ts=20230322191221}} {{AFC comment|1=The prose needs to be improved significantly so that it has a formal, encyclopaedic tone. I would also recommend adding more references. [[User:InterstellarGamer12321|InterstellarGamer12321]] ([[User talk:InterstellarGamer12321|talk]] | [[Special:Contributions/InterstellarGamer12321|contribs]]) 07:54, 23 March 2023 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Shinto creator god}} {{Draft topics|biography|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} {{draft}} [[File:Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|Umashiashikabihikoji|ja|ウマシアシカビヒコヂ}} is a Japanese primordial deity.{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}} He was the fourth one to come into existence His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto. The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto. He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}} He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]]. Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji. [[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by {{ill|Amenotokotachi|ja|天之常立神}}. He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe. He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor == In the Kojiki == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name. The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away. == Nihongi Variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot. The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs. ==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[:Category:Shinto kami]] [[:Category:Androgynous and hermaphroditic deities]] [[:Category:Amatsukami]] [[:Category:Japanese deities]] [[:Category:Creator deities]] [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:LGBT themes in mythology]] }}

He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto. The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto. He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}} He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]]. Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji. [[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by {{ill|Amenotokotachi|ja|天之常立神}}. He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe. He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor == In the Kojiki == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name. The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away. == Nihongi Variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot. The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs. ==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[:Category:Shinto kami]] [[:Category:Androgynous and hermaphroditic deities]] [[:Category:Amatsukami]] [[:Category:Japanese deities]] [[:Category:Creator deities]] [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:LGBT themes in mythology]] }}

revision.comment: Cleaning up accepted [[Wikipedia:Articles for creation|Articles for creation]] submission ([[WP:AFCH|AFCH]] 0.9.1)
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg|frame]] {{ill|Umashiashikabihikoji|ja|ウマシアシカビヒコヂ}} is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by {{ill|Amenotokotachi|ja|天之常立神}}.<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor<ref name=":0" />
== In the Kojiki == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi Variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by {{ill|Amenotokotachi|ja|天之常立神}}.<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor<ref name=":0" />
== In the Kojiki == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi Variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}}

== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

[[File:Creation_myths_of_Japan-eng.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by {{ill|Amenotokotachi|ja|天之常立神}}.<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />

The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />

[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />

Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Amenotokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />

They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Amenotokotachi. Amenotokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />

The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

{{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}}
]] ]] ]] ]] ]]

[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}}
]] ]] ]] ]] ]]

They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />

This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] {{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}} ]]]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Androgynous and hermaphroditic deities]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Creator deities]] [[Category:Japanese gods]]
}}

[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />

== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />

The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a [[:en:Hitorigami|Hitorigami]], or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}} ]]]]

The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}} ]]]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] {{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami}}, or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}} ]]]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[fr:Umashiashikabihikoji]][[ja:ウマシアシカビヒコヂ]][[zh:可美葦牙彥舅尊]] {{Short description|Shinto creator god}}
[[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] '''Umashiashikabihikoji''' is a Japanese primordial deity.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |date=2011-05-18 |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Concepts of Kami : Hitorigami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518151157/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=10 |archive-date=2011-05-18 |access-date=2023-03-22}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Umashiashikabihikoji • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/umashiashikabihikoji/}}</ref> He was the fourth one to come into existence.<ref name=":2" />
His name in Japanese is 宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神. He is also known as "Fine Budding Reed Lad". He is in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihongi''. These are ancient Japanese texts. He is one of the earliest gods in Shinto.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />
The Kojiki calls this deity Umashiashikabihikoji no kami. The Nihongi refers to it as Umashiashikabihikoji no mikoto.<ref name=":1" /> He is one of the five [[Kotoamatsukami]] according to the [[Kojiki]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Umashiashikabihikoji {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165547/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9740 |archive-date=2023-03-21 |access-date=2023-11-17}}</ref> He is only cited in an "alternate writing" in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref name=":0" />
Umashiashikabihikoji is linked to the creation of heaven and earth. The land was like floating oil at first. It moved like a jellyfish. A reed-like object appeared in this state. It turned into the kami Umashiashikabihikoji.<ref name=":1" />
[[Kamimusubi]] came before him, and he was succeeded by [[Ame-no-Tokotachi]].<ref name=":1" />
He is a {{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}, or a singular divinity born early in the universe.<ref name=":1" />
He was not mentioned as an [[Oyagami]] or clan ancestor.<ref name=":0" />
== In the ''Kojiki'' == The ''Kojiki'' is an old and important Japanese text. It says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth kami to appear. He appeared with other deities like Ametokotachi. They hid themselves after appearing. Umashiashikabihikoji has a gendered name. He is the first kami with such a name.<ref name=":2" />
The Kojiki says Umashiashikabihikoji was the fourth [[kotoamatsukami]]. The [[kotoamatsukami]] were deities who appeared alone. They are called hitorigami. These deities then hid themselves away.<ref name=":1" />
== Nihongi variations == The ''Nihongi'' is another important text. It has different stories about Umashiashikabihikoji. One story says he formed from a reed shoot. This happened after Heaven and Earth separated. Another story says he was the second kami to appear. He appeared after Ame-no-Tokotachi. Ame-no-Tokotachi also formed from a reed shoot.<ref name=":2" />
The Nihongi gives different stories. In some, Umashiashikabihikoji is the first kami. In others, it is the second. This kami is not linked to any clans. Its role is in the early stages of the world. This shows the variety in Shinto myths. It highlights the connection between nature and the divine in Japanese beliefs.<ref name=":1" />
==References== {{reflist}} ]]]]
